content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

July 20, 1992


Mark T. Boehme
Deputy City Attorney
City of Concord
1950 Parkside Drive, MS 08
Concord, CA  94519-2578

Re:  Your Request for Informal Assistance
     Our File No. I-92-206

Dear Mr. Boehme:

     You have requested advice concerning application of the
conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the
"Act")1 to the duties of Mr. Fred Doster, a member of the City of
Concord's Redevelopment Agency Advisory Committee ("RAC").  The
following advice is based upon the facts provided in your letters
and our telephone conversations of May 4 and 27, 1992.

     Your letter does not seek advice regarding a specific pending
governmental decision and your question is general in nature.
Accordingly, we treat your letter as a request for informal
assistance pursuant to the provisions of Regulation 18239.2
Additionally, our advice is prospective in nature and we do not
comment on any past conduct.

QUESTION

     Under the Act, may Mr. Doster participate in various
decisions concerning the Cinema Square redevelopment project?




1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015.  All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.  Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq.  All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Government Code Section 83114; 2 Cal. Code of Regs.
Section 18329(c)(3).)


428 J Street, Suite 800  •  P.O. Box 807  •  Sacramento, CA  95812  •  (916) 322-5660

Our File No. I-92-206
Page 2


CONCLUSION

Mr. Doster may not participate in any decisions regarding the
Cinema Square project which will have a material financial effect
on any of his financial interests, as discussed below.

FACTS

Mr. Doster is a member of the Concord RAC. The redevelopment
agency ("agency") created the RAC for the purpose of reviewing the
formulation and implementation of redevelopment plans and
projects. Your office has concluded that the RAC is a local
government agency (Section 83041) and that Mr. Doster is a public
official (Section 82048).

The RAC and the redevelopment agency have been reviewing a
major project, Cinema Square, which is located within the
redevelopment project area. Cinema Square is a mixed use
development containing a ten-screen theatre complex, retail shops,
offices and residential units. You informed me that there will be
an exclusive agent (not Mr. Doster) for the Cinema Square project
and that Mr. Doster does not intend to pursue business activities
at Cinema Square.

Mr. Doster is a real estate broker and the sole owner of
Contra Costa Properties. Through his business, he conducts
leasing activities at the Park and Shop Center, which is located
across the street from the proposed Cinema Square complex. The
Park and Shop Center is also in the redevelopment area, but there
is no redevelopment currently planned for this center.

Mr. Doster is also an officer on the nonprofit board of
directors of the Park and Shop Center. The board pays him a
management fee of $2,500 a month for his management duties at Park
and Shop. Mr. Doster does not have any ownership interest in the
Park and Shop Center.

ANALYSIS

I have enclosed an attachment outlining the general rules of
conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act. Section 87100
prohibits a public official from making, participating in making,
or otherwise using his official position to influence a
governmental decision in which he has a financial interest.
Mr. Doster may not participate in any decisions regarding the
Cinema Square project if it is reasonably foreseeable that the
decision will have a material financial effect on his real estate
business, Park and Shop or any other financial interest. (Section
87103(a),(b) and (c).)

Our File No. I-92-206
Page 3


You have not requested advice pertaining to a specific
decision. It appears that Mr. Doster has several financial
interests which may be indirectly involved in any decision
regarding the Cinema Square project. If it is reasonably
foreseeable that there will be a material financial effect on any
of these interests, Mr. Doster may not participate in the
decision.

Foreseeability

Whether the financial effects of a decision are reasonably
foreseeable at the time a governmental decision is made depends on
the facts of each particular case. An effect is considered
reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial likelihood that
it will occur. Certainty is not required. However, if an effect
is only a mere possibility, it is not reasonably foreseeable.
(Downey Cares v. Downey Community Development Commission (1987)
196 Cal.App.3d 938; In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.)

As discussed in the Fargo Advice Letter (No. I-91-089), the
anticipated result of any redevelopment plan is to increase the
property values and improve the business climate within the
project area. The very nature of redevelopment projects has led
the Commission to find that it is reasonably foreseeable that
there will be a financial effect on real property values and
business interests located within or near project areas. (In re
Ogelsby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71; Armento Advice Letter, No. A-90-
499.) If the public official has an interest in real property or
a business located in the redevelopment area, we presume that a
financial effect on these interests is foreseeable.

In the Ogelsby Opinion, the Commission concluded that the
proposed redevelopment project would have a material financial
effect on the official's real estate business because such a
business earns its income from commissions based on a percentage
of the value of the property. With redevelopment, the property
value increases and with such increases, the amount of the
commission income increases.

Materiality

Mr. Doster must disqualify himself from participating in any
decisions regarding the Cinema Square project if the foreseeable
effect on any of his economic interests is material. The
Commission has adopted a series of regulations which provide
guidance concerning whether the reasonably foreseeable financial
effects of a decision are material. (Regulation 18702.) These
regulations apply different standards depending on whether the
decision will directly or indirectly affect the official's
economic interests.

Our File No. I-92-206
Page 4


     To determine if there will be a material financial effect on
Contra Costa Properties, Mr. Doster's real estate business,
Regulation 18702.2(g) (copy enclosed) provides guidance. For
example, if development of the Cinema Square project would cause
the Park and Shop Center to either lower or increase its rents and
this change would materially affect Mr. Doster's commission
income, Mr. Doster may not participate in the Cinema Square
decisions. Or, if it is foreseeable that tenants will move from
the Park and Shop Center to the Cinema Square project and there
will be a material effect on Mr. Doster's business, he may not
participate.

     You stated that Mr. Doster does not have any ownership
interest in the Park and Shop Center. However, you have not
provided us with information on whether Mr. Doster has any other
economic interests in the center, for example, a leasehold
interest. (See Section 82033 and Regulation 18233.) Regulation
18702.4 gives guidance in determining if a decision will have a
material financial effect on a leasehold interest.

     The Park and Shop Center is a source of income to Mr. Doster
of approximately $2,500 a month, which is payment for his
management services. You have stated that he is paid this
management fee by the nonprofit board of directors. If, in fact,
the nonprofit entity is the source of income to Mr. Doster, you
must consult Regulation 18702.5 to determine the material
financial effect on the nonprofit entity. However, if the Park
and Shop Center, a business entity, is actually the source of
income to Mr. Doster, you must then consult Regulation 18702.2 to
determine materiality.

     As a real estate broker, Mr. Doster's clients are also a
source of income to him. (Regulation 18704.3). If there will be
a material financial effect on any of his clients (Regulations
18702.6 and/or 18702.2) as a result of any of the decisions,
Mr. Doster must also disqualify from these decisions.

     Finally, we make no comment on any of Mr. Doster's past
conduct. It appears that the redevelopment plans has already been
adopted and the current decisions deal with the implementation of
the Cinema Square project. However, you have not provided
sufficient information about the redevelopment project and the
nature of the pending decisions. Therefore, it is incumbent upon
you to analyze each decision to determine if there will be a
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on any of
Mr. Doster's financial interests.

Our File No. I-92-206
Page 5


I trust this letter answers your question.³

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]
                                        Jill Stecher
                                        Counsel, Legal Division

SH:JS:aa

Enclosure




³ Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries. Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of 10¢ per page.
